attacking_style: Attacks on key climbs 3-6km from finish or initiates long-range moves (66km) on marginal crosswinds/cobbles. Prefers bridging to breakaways late rather than leading chase, then relies on superior finishing kick within final 400m.
team_tactics: Quick-Step maximized numerical advantage through early attacks to isolate rivals (Liège, Flanders). Later with Lotto, initiated coordinated twin attacks but lacked team depth to sustain echelon splits. Willing to sacrifice personal ambitions for team leaders.
terrain_preference: Excels on steep gradient finishes (3km+ climbs), cobbled classics requiring sustained power, and technical corner-heavy finishes. Cauberg-type punchy climbs suit his tactical patience and finishing speed perfectly. Crosswind positioning instinct remains sharp.
vulnerability: Insufficient power reserve when attacking solo outside sprint trains (caught 350m out). Struggles in pure reduced bunch sprints against fresher specialists. Team depth issues post-Quick-Step limit ability to capitalize on correctly-read race situations.
key_patterns: Bridges to breakaways on final climb rather than early marking, avoiding premature attention (Vuelta, Amstel).
Times finishing kick within final 100-400m with exceptional precision across multiple wins (Vuelta sprint, Amstel, Paris-Roubaix).
Initiates attacks on iconic climbs (Oude Kwaremont, Roche-aux-Faucons) to fracture peloton for team advantage.
Capable of extended 1.5km leadout duties despite veteran status and classics specialization (Tour de Suisse, Guangxi).
Reads crosswinds correctly but execution limited by team support post-Quick-Step era (Paris-Nice 2021).
Maintains competitive edge at 38+ years through tactical intelligence over pure power (coordinated attacks 2021).
form_trajectory: negative: 2021 observations show correct tactical instincts (crosswind reading, coordinated attacks) undermined by insufficient team depth and likely declining physical capacity at age 38. Last win data from 2019.
